mnemonics.title: DEPENDENT VARIABLE
mnemonics.content: DEPENDENT VARIABLE = THE VARIABLE THAT DEPENDS ON OTHERS. Think: D in Dependent stands for DEFINITELY THE ONE WE PREDICT.

mnemonics.title: BIAS-VARIANCE BALANCE
mnemonics.content: BIAS = BIG MISTAKES FROM SIMPLE THINKING. VARIANCE = VARYING WIDELY WITH SMALL CHANGES. Balance is key to avoid UNDERFIT or OVERFIT.

mnemonics.title: OVERFITTING MEMORY
mnemonics.content: OVERFITTING = MODEL THAT OVER-LEARNS TRAINING NOISE, LIKE MEMORIZING EXAM ANSWERS WITHOUT UNDERSTANDING CONCEPTS.

mnemonics.title: RESIDUAL REMEMBER
mnemonics.content: RESIDUAL = REMEMBER IT'S THE ACTUAL (A) MINUS PREDICTED (P), SO RESIDUAL = A - P. ALWAYS A BEFORE P IN ALPHABET!

acronyms.usage: Measures average squared difference between predicted and actual values. Lower MSE = better model.
Square root of MSE, brings error back to original units for easier interpretation.
Average absolute differences, less sensitive to outliers than MSE.
Proportion of variance explained by model. Values from 0 to 1, higher is better.
Total squared differences between predicted and actual values, minimized in regression.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine fitting a rubber band through data points. The best fit is when the rubber band is stretched tightest with equal pull from all points.
Draw a simple line (underfit), a perfect curve through all points (overfit), and a smooth curve capturing general trend (good fit). Like under-drawing, over-drawing, and sketching the essential form.
Think of throwing darts: HIGH BIAS = consistently hitting off-target in one direction. HIGH VARIANCE = scattered all over. LOW BOTH = clustered near bullseye.
If residuals form a funnel shape, there's heteroscedasticity. If they form a U-shape, model is non-linear. Random scatter = good model.

analogies.analogy: Like a trend line showing how average house prices change with house size - it doesn't predict exact prices but shows the general direction.
Like a GPS that calculates distance from destination. Each step (parameter adjustment) tries to minimize distance to optimal solution.
Like measuring height in centimeters and weight in kilograms - both need normalization to compare fairly, just like in regression algorithms.
Like a student who studies textbook examples (training) and can solve new problems (testing), not just memorize exact questions.
